title: Listening - Part 4: Facing a Fear
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.
topic: five people talking about how they overcame a specific fear and the effect it had on them

content.audio_script: Speaker 1: I’d had a paralyzing fear of public speaking since school, but [21]when I was asked to give a speech at my sister’s wedding, I knew I couldn't let her down[/21]. I took a three-month course to prepare. On the day, I was shaking, but I did it. [26]The real surprise was the massive boost in my professional confidence that followed[/26]. I started contributing much more in meetings and even applied for a promotion that I would have previously avoided. It turns out that once you’ve faced your biggest social fear, everything else feels manageable. || Speaker 2: I’d always been terrified of heights, so [22]I signed up for a bungee jump primarily to prove to myself that I wasn't a coward[/22]. I wanted to reclaim my sense of bravery. Standing on that ledge was the hardest thing I’ve ever done. [27]What I didn't expect was the profound sense of mental clarity I felt immediately after the jump[/27]. All my daily worries seemed to vanish for weeks. It was like my brain had been reset. It’s made me much more decisive in my personal life, as I no longer over-analyze every small risk. || Speaker 3: I’d developed a fear of driving after a minor accident years ago. [23]I decided to take advanced driving lessons because I was tired of the inconvenience of relying on public transport[/23] in a rural area. I needed my independence back. It was a slow process, but [28]I was surprised by how much I actually began to enjoy the sense of control that driving gives me[/28]. I now find long drives quite relaxing, which is something I never thought I’d say. It’s given me a whole new sense of freedom and autonomy that has improved my overall happiness. || Speaker 4: I’ve always been scared of the dark, which is embarrassing for an adult! [24]I finally decided to tackle it by going on a guided night-time trek in the jungle because I wanted to experience the world in a different way[/24]. It was terrifying but beautiful. [29]The most rewarding part has been the way it’s changed my perspective on what I’m capable of enduring[/29]. I realized that fear is often just an overactive imagination. Now, when I face a challenge at work, I remind myself of that night in the jungle and I feel much more resilient and capable. || Speaker 5: I’d always been afraid of water, so [25]I took up swimming lessons as a way to bond with my children who love being in the pool[/25]. I didn't want my fear to hold them back. It was a long road, but [30]the most significant outcome has been the wonderful new social circle I’ve found at the local pool[/30]. I’ve met a group of other adults who were also late learners, and we’ve become great friends. It’s given me a sense of belonging and a new hobby that I genuinely look forward to every week. It’s transformed my social life in a way I never anticipated.

task1.title: Task One
task1.instruction: For questions 21–25, choose from the list (A–H) what motivated each speaker to face their fear.

questions.speaker: Speaker 1
questions.explanation: Speaker 1 didn't want to let her sister down at her wedding.

questions.speaker: Speaker 2
questions.explanation: Speaker 2 wanted to prove to himself that he wasn't a coward and reclaim his bravery.

questions.speaker: Speaker 3
questions.explanation: Speaker 3 was tired of the inconvenience of relying on public transport.

questions.speaker: Speaker 4
questions.explanation: Speaker 4 wanted to experience the world in a different way.

questions.speaker: Speaker 5
questions.explanation: Speaker 5 wanted to bond with her children who love being in the pool.

options.C: to share an activity with their kids
options.D: to meet a requirement for their job
options.E: to win a bet with a close friend
options.G: to gain a new perspective on nature
options.H: to recover from a recent traumatic event

task2.title: Task Two
task2.instruction: For questions 26–30, choose from the list (A–H) what each speaker mentions as an outcome of facing their fear.

questions.speaker: Speaker 1
questions.explanation: Speaker 1 mentions a massive boost in professional confidence and applying for a promotion.

questions.speaker: Speaker 2
questions.explanation: Speaker 2 mentions a sense of mental clarity and becoming more decisive.

questions.speaker: Speaker 3
questions.explanation: Speaker 3 was surprised by how much she began to enjoy the sense of control.

questions.speaker: Speaker 4
questions.explanation: Speaker 4 mentions a change in his perspective on what he is capable of enduring and feeling more resilient.

questions.speaker: Speaker 5
questions.explanation: Speaker 5 highlights the new social circle and friendships she made at the pool.

options.A: an increased level of mental toughness
options.B: a desire to teach others the same skill
options.C: a newfound interest in extreme sports
options.D: the discovery of a relaxing new pastime
options.E: a more direct approach to decision-making
options.F: the resolution of a long-standing physical issue
options.H: a positive impact on their career path
